title: 5 Exercises to Strengthen Your Knee Before Surgery
excerpt: Discover safe, low-impact movements designed to build joint stability and improve recovery outcomes after knee surgery.
content:
Pre-operative physical therapy, often called "pre-hab," is one of the most effective ways to accelerate your recovery after knee surgery. By strengthening the primary supporting muscles around the knee joint—specifically the quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes—you give your joint a stable foundation that protects it during post-operative healing.

Here are 5 safe, low-impact pre-hab exercises recommended by Dr. Manish Bansal's - The Joint Clinic:

1. Quadriceps Sets (Quad Sets)

Lie on your back with your leg straight. Tighten the muscle on the top of your thigh by pushing the back of your knee down into the bed. Hold for 5–10 seconds, then release. Perform 3 sets of 10 repetitions daily.

2. Straight Leg Raises

Lie on your back, bend your non-surgical leg, and keep your surgical leg straight. Slowly lift the straight leg about 12 inches off the ground, hold for 3 seconds, then lower it slowly. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.

3. Glute Bridges

Lie on your back with both knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Squeeze your buttocks and lift your hips toward the ceiling until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees. Hold for 5 seconds and lower. Perform 2 sets of 10 reps.

4. Standing Hamstring Curls

Stand holding onto a stable chair or counter. Slowly bend your knee, bringing your heel up toward your buttocks as far as comfortable. Hold for 2 seconds and slowly lower. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.

5. Clamshells

Lie on your side with your hips and knees bent, heels together. Keep your feet touching and slowly raise your top knee open like a clamshell. Keep your pelvis stable without rotating backward. Hold for 2 seconds and lower. Perform 2 sets of 15 reps.

Always perform these movements slowly and stop immediately if you feel sharp pain. Consulting with Dr. Manish Bansal before starting any pre-hab program ensures these exercises align with your specific knee condition.

title: How to Prepare for a Shoulder Arthroscopy
excerpt: A step-by-step preparation checklist covering pre-operative tests, home setup, and what to expect on procedure day.
content:
Shoulder arthroscopy is a minimally invasive keyhole procedure used to diagnose and treat joint issues like rotator cuff tears, labral tears, and shoulder impingement. While the procedure itself has minimal recovery time compared to open surgery, proper preparation at home is key to a smooth and comfortable healing process.

Follow this pre-operative preparation checklist from The Joint Clinic:

1. Medical Clearance & Medication Audit

Complete any pre-surgical blood tests, ECG, or medical evaluations requested. Share a list of all current medications, especially blood thinners, with Dr. Bansal, as some must be paused before surgery.

2. Arrange Post-Operative Support

You will be discharged in a shoulder sling, which means you cannot drive. Arrange for a family member or friend to drive you home and assist you with daily tasks like meal prep or dressing for the first 24–48 hours.

3. Prepare Your Home Environment

Since lifting and reaching will be limited, place frequently used items (plates, cups, toiletries, clothes) at waist level. Buy slip-on shoes and loose-fitting shirts that button up in the front so you do not have to pull clothing over your head.

4. Plan for Sleeping Comfort

Sleeping flat on your back after shoulder surgery can be uncomfortable. Many patients find it easiest to sleep in a semi-reclined position. Prepare a comfortable recliner chair or buy a wedge pillow to prop yourself up in bed.

5. Fasting Guidelines

Do not eat or drink anything after midnight on the night before your surgery, unless explicitly directed otherwise by the clinical staff.

title: Understanding Knee Osteoarthritis & Joint Care
excerpt: Learn about the symptoms of osteoarthritis, protective joint care practices, and non-surgical management programs.
content:
Knee osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by the gradual wear and tear of the protective cartilage that cushions the ends of your bones. When cartilage wears down, the bones rub closer together, leading to friction, inflammation, stiffness, and pain.

While osteoarthritis is progressive, there are several highly effective ways to manage the condition and delay or avoid the need for joint replacement surgery:

1. Low-Impact Aerobic Exercise

Exercises like swimming, stationary cycling, and water aerobics keep the joint mobile and strengthen the surrounding muscles without placing heavy impact shock on the knee joint.

2. Weight Management

Every extra pound of body weight adds four pounds of pressure on the knee joint during walking. Maintaining a healthy weight significantly reduces joint stress and relieves pain.

3. Bracing & Orthotics

Specialized unloader braces can shift weight away from the damaged side of the knee joint, providing immediate pain relief during walking. Custom shoe inserts may also help align your foot structure.

4. Healthy Joint Nutrition

Focus on an anti-inflammatory diet rich in Omega-3 fatty acids (fish, walnuts, flaxseeds) and antioxidants (berries, leafy greens) to support overall joint health and cartilage support.

5. Consulting a Specialist

If pain persists despite lifestyle adjustments, consult Dr. Manish Bansal. Options such as joint injections, physical therapy, or minimally invasive procedures can help manage symptoms and preserve your joint health.
